feedback.text: [Confirmshaming and addictive design] Hello, I would like to give a feed back on confirm shaming. I understand it can be a dark practice in many cases. Sometimes, it is used in friendly way, like in the game Doom (see the pdf - there are different dialog windows, I chose 2 to limit the size of the document), where it is an innocent joke. I am not sure how would a law distinguish between sarcastic use and real manipulation. I think this problematic is more nuanced and much easier to resist than scrollhijackikng or technological barriers like calling on support for cancelation. Shouldnt be those topics in different categories depending on their severity? Speaking about manipulation, sometimes it is welcomed, like in Duolingo, which uses different tricks to force me to practice my Dutch and German. Thats why decided to use the app as motivation. (I also added one example.) Similarly fitness apps use tricks to motivate people to exercise more. Surely, the app benefits from it, but also the user and also our healthcare system. I think being addicted on learning German and daily burning calories is a good thing. Will the law distinguish between the categories, where it is done with malice and where the user benefits? Thank you for reading this feedback. With regards, Matouš Pikous
